recommendations.rationale: First-line investigation. Evaluates gallbladder, liver, spleen, kidneys, free fluid, appendix (limited), and pelvic organs. Rapid, bedside feasible.
recommendations.india_context: Available at secondary/district hospital level. Preferred first-line, especially in females of reproductive age.
recommendations.special_notes: Limited by bowel gas in some cases. If USG inconclusive, proceed to CT.

recommendations.rationale: Most comprehensive evaluation of acute abdomen. Detects perforation, obstruction, appendicitis, vascular events, and solid organ pathology.
recommendations.india_context: Indicated when USG inconclusive or clinical severity warrants rapid diagnosis. Reduces unnecessary surgery.
recommendations.special_notes: Consider oral + IV contrast protocol for bowel detail.

recommendations.rationale: Quick, widely available. Detects pneumoperitoneum (free air under diaphragm), bowel obstruction (air-fluid levels), calculi.
recommendations.india_context: Available even at PHC level. Always include erect CXR for subdiaphragmatic free air.
recommendations.special_notes: Sensitivity for free air ~55%. CT is more accurate.

recommendations.rationale: Alternative when CT contraindicated. Particularly useful in pregnancy for appendicitis workup.
recommendations.india_context: Preferred over CT in pregnancy. Limited availability at secondary level.

recommendations.rationale: First-line, especially in children and females. Avoids radiation. Sensitivity 75–90% in expert hands.
recommendations.india_context: Mandatory first step in paediatric patients and women of reproductive age. Graded compression technique.
recommendations.special_notes: Non-visualisation of appendix on USG does not exclude appendicitis. Proceed to CT if inconclusive.

recommendations.rationale: Highest sensitivity and specificity (>95%). Detects perforation, abscess, and alternative diagnoses.
recommendations.india_context: Recommended when USG inconclusive or clinical score is equivocal (Alvarado 5–6). Reduces negative appendicectomy rate.
recommendations.special_notes: Alvarado score >7: go to OT. Score 5–6: CT indicated.
contraindicated_if: pregnancy (use MRI or USG + clinical decision)

recommendations.rationale: Preferred alternative in pregnancy. High sensitivity for appendicitis without radiation.
recommendations.india_context: Use when CT contraindicated (pregnancy). Requires 24/7 MRI access — consider availability.

scenarios.title: Acute Cholecystitis / Biliary Colic
references: ACR AC Right Upper Quadrant Pain 2022

recommendations.rationale: Definitive first-line investigation. Detects gallstones (>95% sensitivity), wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, CBD dilatation, Murphy sign.
recommendations.india_context: Widely available. Highly accurate for gallstones. Fasting for 4–6 hours improves gallbladder distension.
recommendations.special_notes: Includes assessment of CBD diameter. CBD >8 mm suggests choledocholithiasis.

recommendations.rationale: For complications — gangrenous cholecystitis, perforation, pericholecystic abscess, Mirizzi syndrome.
recommendations.india_context: Use only if USG inconclusive or complications suspected.
recommendations.special_notes: CT detects emphysematous cholecystitis (gas in gallbladder wall) — surgical emergency.

recommendations.rationale: For suspected choledocholithiasis (CBD stones). Non-invasive alternative to ERCP for diagnosis.
recommendations.india_context: Preferred non-invasive investigation for CBD stones before ERCP. Available at tertiary centres.
recommendations.special_notes: ERCP reserved for therapeutic intervention after MRCP confirmation.

recommendations.rationale: For equivocal cholecystitis when USG inconclusive. Detects cystic duct obstruction (non-filling of gallbladder).
recommendations.india_context: Limited availability. Use only when diagnosis remains uncertain after USG and CT.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard. Detects nearly all stones (>95% sensitivity) including radiolucent uric acid stones missed on plain X-ray.
recommendations.india_context: Investigation of choice. Low-dose CT KUB protocol recommended to reduce radiation.
recommendations.special_notes: Also detects alternative diagnoses (appendicitis, aortic aneurysm). No contrast required for stones.

recommendations.rationale: First-line in pregnancy and paediatrics. Detects hydronephrosis and large renal stones well. Misses ureteric stones.
recommendations.india_context: Use as first-line in pregnant women. Widely available. Pair with X-ray KUB.
recommendations.special_notes: Sensitivity for ureteric stones only ~20–50%. CT preferred for definitive diagnosis.

recommendations.rationale: Detects radio-opaque stones (calcium oxalate, struvite). Useful for follow-up of known stones.
recommendations.india_context: Widely available, low cost. 20% of urinary stones are radiolucent (uric acid) and will be missed.
recommendations.special_notes: Uric acid and cystine stones not visible on plain X-ray.

recommendations.rationale: Preferred in pregnancy when CT is contraindicated. Detects hydronephrosis and signs of obstruction.
recommendations.india_context: Use in pregnant women when USG inconclusive.
recommendations.special_notes: Cannot directly visualise most stones, but detects ureteric oedema and obstructive changes.

recommendations.rationale: First-line investigation. Identifies biliary dilation, gallstones, liver parenchymal disease, and mass lesions.
recommendations.india_context: Mandatory first step. Distinguishes obstructive from hepatocellular jaundice. Widely available.
recommendations.special_notes: CBD >8 mm suggests obstruction. Intrahepatic biliary dilation points to high obstruction.

recommendations.rationale: Non-invasive gold standard for biliary anatomy, CBD stones, strictures, and PSC. Equivalent to ERCP for diagnosis.
recommendations.india_context: Preferred over diagnostic ERCP. Available at tertiary care centres. No radiation.
recommendations.special_notes: Cannot be therapeutic. Proceed to ERCP only after diagnostic confirmation.

recommendations.rationale: For characterising pancreatic/biliary mass, hepatic metastases, vascular involvement — staging for resectability.
recommendations.india_context: Essential for surgical planning in suspected cholangiocarcinoma or pancreatic head carcinoma.
recommendations.special_notes: Triphasic protocol: arterial, portal venous, delayed phases for liver and pancreas.

recommendations.rationale: First-line to identify gallstone aetiology (cholecystolithiasis, CBD stones). Detects peri-pancreatic fluid.
recommendations.india_context: Immediate USG to rule out gallstone pancreatitis — changes management.
recommendations.special_notes: Pancreas may be poorly visualised due to bowel gas in acute setting. CT is more definitive.

recommendations.rationale: For severity assessment (CTSI — CT Severity Index), necrosis detection, complications (abscess, pseudocyst, vascular).
recommendations.india_context: Perform at 48–72 hours in moderate-severe pancreatitis. Not needed in mild cases.
recommendations.special_notes: CTSI (Balthazar + Necrosis score) predicts morbidity and mortality. Avoid in first 48h (underestimates necrosis).

recommendations.rationale: Alternative to CT for severity staging, especially for detecting duct disruption and CBD stones without radiation.
recommendations.india_context: Use when CT contraindicated. MRCP identifies bile duct stones or strictures.

recommendations.rationale: Limited value in pancreatitis. May show sentinel loop, colon cut-off sign, or calcification in chronic disease.
recommendations.india_context: Perform to exclude perforation/obstruction initially. Not diagnostic for pancreatitis.

recommendations.rationale: First-line surveillance and initial characterisation. Identifies hepatic mass, portal vein thrombosis, cirrhosis features, and ascites. Doppler for vascularity.
recommendations.india_context: HBV and HCV-related cirrhosis are major HCC risk factors in India. Ultrasound surveillance every 6 months is recommended for all cirrhotic patients. AFP combined with ultrasound for screening.
recommendations.special_notes: Surveillance US in cirrhosis: every 6 months. Any new nodule >1 cm in cirrhotic liver needs CT/MRI for characterisation (LI-RADS).

recommendations.rationale: Standard for HCC diagnosis and staging. Arterial hyperenhancement with portal venous washout is diagnostic (LI-RADS ≥4). Non-invasive diagnosis of HCC possible in cirrhotic liver.
recommendations.india_context: Most cost-effective diagnostic tool for HCC in India. LI-RADS classification used at oncology centres. Evaluate portal vein, hepatic veins, and adjacent structures for resectability.
recommendations.special_notes: LI-RADS criteria: arterial phase hyperenhancement + washout + capsule = LI-RADS 5 = definite HCC. LIRADS 3-4 may need biopsy.

recommendations.rationale: Most sensitive for HCC detection, especially small lesions (<2 cm). Hepatobiliary phase (EOB-MRI) with hypointense defect is highly specific for HCC. Superior to CT for indeterminate lesions.
recommendations.india_context: Available at large hepatology and oncology centres. EOB-Primovist (gadoxetate) is the preferred agent. Used for surgical planning and transplant eligibility assessment.
recommendations.special_notes: DWI restricted diffusion + arterial enhancement + HBP hypointensity = HCC. Best tool for detecting multifocal small HCCs before transplant listing.

recommendations.rationale: HCC most commonly metastasises to lung. CT chest is essential for staging before surgical resection or liver transplantation evaluation.
recommendations.india_context: Lung and bone are common metastatic sites. Complete BCLC staging requires chest CT.

recommendations.rationale: First-line investigation. Multiple air-fluid levels, dilated bowel loops, paucity of gas distally. Erect view shows fluid levels; supine shows distribution. Rapid, cheap, and widely available.
recommendations.india_context: Mandatory first investigation in all bowel obstruction in India. Sigmoid volvulus is extremely common in India (high-fibre diet, elderly) — coffee bean sign on X-ray is diagnostic.
recommendations.special_notes: SBO: Dilated small bowel (>3 cm) with air-fluid levels in stepladder pattern. LBO: Dilated large bowel (>6 cm, caecum >9 cm). Sigmoid volvulus: inverted U-loop, coffee bean sign.

recommendations.rationale: Definitive investigation for cause, level, and complications (strangulation, ischaemia, perforation). Identifies transition point, hernia, adhesions, malignancy, and closed-loop obstruction.
recommendations.india_context: CT is preferred when diagnosis is in doubt or surgery is planned. Identifies mesenteric ischaemia (high mortality if missed). Colonic carcinoma is a common cause of LBO in India.
recommendations.special_notes: Closed-loop obstruction: C/U-shaped loop with tapered ends and mesenteric swirl — surgical emergency. Pneumatosis intestinalis = ischaemia.

recommendations.rationale: Can detect dilated loops, peristalsis, and hernia in selected cases. Limited by bowel gas in obstruction. Useful in pregnant patients.
recommendations.india_context: Available everywhere. Use in pregnancy as first-line. Otherwise CT is preferred.

recommendations.rationale: For active GI bleeding in haemodynamically unstable patients or when endoscopy is non-diagnostic. Detects bleeding >0.3 mL/min. Identifies site for targeted embolisation or surgery.
recommendations.india_context: Diverticular disease, angiodysplasia, and colorectal carcinoma are common causes in India. CTA is first-line for brisk lower GI bleeding.
recommendations.special_notes: Perform during active bleeding for maximum sensitivity. Identifies bleeding site for selective mesenteric angiography and embolisation.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for active brisk lower GI bleeding — allows simultaneous diagnosis and treatment (embolisation). Detects bleeding >0.5 mL/min.
recommendations.india_context: Available at interventional radiology centres in India. Superselective embolisation can stop bleeding with <5% bowel ischaemia risk.
recommendations.special_notes: Can treat bleeding with coil, gel-foam, or n-butyl cyanoacrylate embolisation. Requires active bleeding at time of procedure.

recommendations.rationale: Detects intermittent or slow GI bleeding (>0.1 mL/min). Can detect bleeding over 24 hours and localise site before angiography. Useful when bleeding is too slow for CTA/DSA.
recommendations.india_context: Available at nuclear medicine centres. Useful for obscure GI bleeding workup.
recommendations.special_notes: High sensitivity for slow bleeding but poor spatial localisation. Use to confirm active bleeding before angiography.

scenarios.title: Amoebic Liver Abscess / Tropical Pyogenic Liver Abscess

recommendations.rationale: First-line investigation. Identifies abscess cavity, size, location, echogenicity, and wall features. Also guides percutaneous aspiration/drainage. Amoebic abscess appears as round, well-defined hypoechoic mass.
recommendations.india_context: Amoebic liver abscess is among the most common causes of liver abscess in tropical India. Often presents with fever, right hypochondrial pain in young males. Serology (IHA/ELISA) confirms amoebic aetiology. Right lobe most common.
recommendations.special_notes: Amoebic: single, right lobe, peripheral, hypoechoic. Pyogenic: may be multiple, septated. Aspiration if >10 cm or failing to respond in 72 hours.

recommendations.rationale: For complex or multiloculated abscesses, uncertain diagnosis, suspected complications (rupture into pleura, pericardium), or when ultrasound is limited.
recommendations.india_context: CT is used when the abscess is large, rupture is suspected, or there are multiple abscesses suggesting pyogenic aetiology. Complications (pleural empyema, pericarditis) require CT.
recommendations.special_notes: Rim-enhancing hypoattenuating mass with perilesional oedema. Intraabdominal rupture: free fluid, peritonitis.

recommendations.rationale: Right dome of diaphragm elevation, right pleural effusion, and hepatomegaly may suggest liver abscess. Useful to screen for complications.
recommendations.india_context: Simple, available, and quick. Elevated right hemidiaphragm is an important early sign of liver abscess.
